cards.front: What is the ONE sentence definition of Factory Pattern?
cards.back: Move object creation into a dedicated place so clients don't know which concrete class is instantiated.

cards.front: What are the 3 flavors of Factory?
cards.back:
1. Simple Factory — separate class with switch/if-else
2. Registry — Map<Type, Supplier> (zero if-else)
3. Factory Method — abstract method in base class, subclasses override

cards.front: When should you use Simple Factory vs Factory Method?
cards.back: Simple Factory: when ONE place decides (80% of cases). Factory Method: when SUBCLASSES need to decide what to create — the creator is polymorphic.

cards.front: What triggers Abstract Factory? (vs Simple Factory)
cards.back: When objects form a FAMILY that must vary TOGETHER. Tax + Receipt + Currency per region. Simple Factory creates ONE product, Abstract Factory creates a FAMILY.
Factory creates via interface → in tests, inject mock factory that returns stub objects. No real network calls, no real databases. Instant, isolated unit tests.

cards.front: You see 'new ConcreteClass()' in 5 different service files. Pattern?
cards.back: Factory (Simple). Centralize creation so adding new types = ONE file change.

cards.front: A base class defines an algorithm but defers object creation to subclasses. Pattern?
cards.back: Factory Method. The base class has a template method that calls an abstract createX() method. Subclasses override createX() to return their specific product.

cards.front: Indian orders need GST + ₹ formatter + Indian receipt. US orders need Sales Tax + $ formatter + US receipt. Pattern?
cards.back: Abstract Factory. The family (tax + formatter + receipt) must vary TOGETHER per region. One factory per region creates the whole family.

cards.front: switch(type) { case A: return new A(); case B: return new B(); ... } — How to eliminate the switch?
cards.back: Registry pattern: Map<String, Supplier<Product>>. Register creators in a map, create() becomes map.get(type).get(). Adding type = adding one registration line.

cards.front: You need to create objects at RUNTIME based on user input or config. Is DI enough?
cards.back: No. DI resolves at STARTUP. Factory resolves at RUNTIME. Use BOTH: DI injects the Factory bean, Factory creates the right object based on runtime data.
A Factory that creates EVERYTHING — notifications, payments, users, orders. It becomes a 2000-line switch statement. Fix: one Factory per product family (SRP).

cards.front: What's wrong with Factory Method when you only have one creator?
cards.back: Over-engineering. If there's only ONE subclass, there's no polymorphism. Use Simple Factory instead. Factory Method shines with 3+ subclasses that need different creation logic.

cards.front: Client calls factory.create() then immediately casts to concrete type. Is this OK?
cards.back: NO. This defeats the purpose. If client knows the concrete type, it doesn't need a factory. Fix: make the interface rich enough that casting is never needed.

cards.front: Abstract Factory where one factory creates unrelated objects (User + Payment + Logger). Is this right?
cards.back: NO. Abstract Factory is for objects that form a FAMILY — they MUST be used together. User, Payment, Logger are independent. Use separate Simple Factories.

cards.front: How does Factory relate to SRP (Single Responsibility)?
cards.back: Factory extracts creation responsibility OUT of business services. OrderService's job is processing orders, not creating notifications. Factory gives creation its own class.

cards.front: How does Registry pattern satisfy OCP (Open/Closed)?
cards.back: Adding new type = register() call. NO modification to Factory class. The class is CLOSED for modification but OPEN for extension via registration.

cards.front: How does Factory relate to DIP (Dependency Inversion)?
cards.back: Client depends on Notification interface, not EmailNotification class. High-level modules (services) depend on abstractions (interfaces), not low-level modules (concrete classes).
